result:
If I could choose a single superpower, it wouldn't be flight or invisibility—it would be the ability to truly see the world through another's eyes, if only for a moment. Not just their physical sight, but the entire lens of their experience: the memories that color a street corner, the private meaning of a faded song, the unique weight they carry in their chest on an ordinary Tuesday.

We navigate life encased in the beautiful, lonely fortress of our own perception. We make grand pronouncements about human nature, politics, or art, often forgetting that our "truth" is built from a utterly singular collage of sensory data, childhood lessons, heartbreaks, and joys. The stranger who cut you off in traffic might be rushing to a dying parent's bedside. The colleague who seems dismissive may be battling a silent storm of anxiety.

This imagined power isn't about agreement. It's about the dissolution of the abstract "other." It’s the profound, humbling realization that every person you pass is the protagonist of an epic, intricate story you will never fully read. Their inner world is as vast, nuanced, and urgent as your own.

Perhaps, then, the real magic isn't in a fantastical ability, but in the quiet, conscious choice to assume that hidden depth exists. To grant the grace of complexity to everyone we meet. In doing so, we don't just understand others better—we expand the very boundaries of our own humanity, one considered moment at a time. The world becomes less a collection of obstacles and opinions, and more a living library of priceless, walking stories.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
